    Home › Aircraft › Airbus A380

    Airbus A380

    Airbus · Widebody · Out of production (2021) — in service

    The largest passenger aircraft ever built.

    Length72.72 m
    Wingspan79.75 m
    Range14,800 km
    MTOW575 t
    Typical seats525
    Built251
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Seats per metre#1 of 35
    7.22 seats/m typical seats ÷ fuselage length
    Range per tonne#59 of 65
    25.7 km/t maximum range ÷ MTOW in tonnes
    Span-to-length ratio#11 of 68
    1.10 wingspan ÷ fuselage length
    Weight per metre#2 of 68
    7.91 t/m MTOW ÷ fuselage length
    Range in hours#4 of 65
    15.7 h maximum range ÷ cruise speed
    Weight per seat#3 of 35
    1095 kg MTOW ÷ typical seats
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Airbus A380's wingspan, against things you already know the size of.

    Airbus A38079.75 m
    Football pitch (width)68 m
    Blue whale25 m
    New Routemaster bus11.2 m
    You (probably)1.75 m
    #8 of 68 by length → #2 by weight → #31 by speed →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length72.72 m
    Wingspan79.75 m
    Height24.09 m
    MTOW575,000 kg
    Range14,800 km
    Typical seats525 (3-class)
    Maximum seats853 (exit limit)
    Engines4 × GP7200 / Trent 900
    First flight27 April 2005
    Built251

    Compare it

    Put the Airbus A380 next to something

    vs Airbus A350 →vs Boeing 777 →
    vs Boeing 777 vs Airbus A350 Compare with anything →
    ← PreviousAirbus A350 Next →Airbus Beluga

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    The A380 is the only full-length double-deck airliner ever to enter service — not a hump like the 747, but two complete passenger decks from nose to tail. That single decision drives everything else about it: the 24-metre tail, the 80-metre wingspan that forced airports to rebuild gates, and a maximum weight of 575 tonnes, more than any passenger aircraft before or since.

    Commercially it was a bet that air travel would concentrate into mega-hubs. It mostly didn't — the market moved to smaller twins flying point to point — and production ended in 2021 after 251 aircraft. But the fleet that exists is loved: passengers pick flights to be on it, and Emirates built its entire long-haul model around it.

    In this catalog the A380's double deck shows up directly in the numbers: it carries more seats per metre of fuselage than anything else flying.

    Common questions

    Airbus A380 — the short answers

    How fast is the Airbus A380?
    The Airbus A380 cruises at about 945 km/h — roughly 510 knots, or 587 mph.
    How far can the Airbus A380 fly?
    The Airbus A380 has a maximum range of about 14,800 km (7,991 nautical miles, 9,198 miles). At its cruise speed that is roughly 15.7 hours in the air.
    How many people does the Airbus A380 carry?
    A typical Airbus A380 carries 525 passengers. Exact figures vary with the operator's chosen configuration.
    Is the Airbus A380 still in production?
    Status: Out of production (2021) — in service. The Airbus A380 first flew in 2005, and about 251 have been built.
    How big is the Airbus A380?
    The Airbus A380 is 72.72 m long with a wingspan of 79.75 m and a height of 24.09 m. Its maximum takeoff weight is 575,000 kg.
    Variants

    The Airbus A380 line

    A380-800
    The only production variant. Every A380 flying today is an -800.
    Operators

    Who flies the Airbus A380

    All 10 current operators, verified 2026-07-14. Fleet counts are approximate where marked ≈.

    EKEmiratesBy far the largest A380 fleet ever assembled; around 96 are active, with Emirates targeting 110 by the end of 2026.116✓→ BABritish AirwaysAll 12 back in service from London Heathrow after the pandemic.12→ SQSingapore AirlinesThe launch operator — first A380 flight in the world, Singapore–Sydney, October 2007.12→ QFQantasThe full fleet returned by December 2025 after the largest maintenance program in the airline's history.10→ EYEtihad AirwaysParked the whole fleet in 2020, then reactivated it from 2023 as long-haul demand surged.9→ LHLufthansaOperating from Munich, with cabins being retrofitted for service into the 2030s.8→ QRQatar AirwaysReactivated post-pandemic for peak-demand routes from Doha.8→ KEKorean AirTrans-Pacific trunk routes from Seoul Incheon.≈6→ OZAsiana AirlinesHigh-density Asian routes — fleet future tied to the Korean Air merger.≈6→ NHAll Nippon AirwaysThe Flying Honu trio — sea-turtle liveries dedicated to the Tokyo–Honolulu route.3→

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
